title: Pollo en Mole Chicken Mole Sauce

seo.title: Authentic Chicken Mole Recipe (Pollo en Mole)
seo.metaDescription: Learn to make the best authentic Chicken Mole Recipe (Pollo en Mole) at home! This rich, complex Mexican chicken mole sauce is easier than you think. Get the full recipe!
longTailKeywords: how to make chicken mole sauce
pollo en mole recipe step by step
best chicken mole recipe at home
seo.h1: Authentic Chicken Mole Recipe (Pollo en Mole)
h2Headings: What is Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole)?
Ingredients You Need for Chicken Mole
Step-by-Step Instructions for Pollo en Mole
Pro Tips for the Best Chicken Mole Sauce
Serving Suggestions for Pollo en Mole
Variations and Substitutions for Your Mole
seo.imageAlt: Plated authentic chicken mole recipe with rich dark sauce, sesame seeds, and fresh cilantro, served with rice.
seo.imageCaption: Savor the rich, complex flavors of this traditional Mexican Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole) – perfect for a family dinner!

description: Pollo en Mole is a quintessential Mexican dish featuring tender chicken bathed in a rich, complex sauce. This traditional mole poblano offers a harmonious blend of dried chiles, spices, nuts, and a hint of chocolate, creating an unforgettable culinary experience. Perfect for celebrations or a comforting family meal, this recipe brings the authentic flavors of Mexico right to your table.

ingredients.item: Bone-in, skin-on chicken pieces (thighs and drumsticks)
ingredients.notes: or 2 large chicken breasts, cut in half

instructions.title: Cook the Chicken
instructions.text: In a large pot, place the chicken pieces and cover with water. Add a pinch of salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer until the chicken is cooked through and tender, about 20-25 minutes. Remove chicken, reserving the cooking liquid (broth). Set chicken aside.

instructions.title: Prepare and Rehydrate Chiles
instructions.text: Lightly toast the dried chiles in a dry skillet over medium heat for 1-2 minutes per side until fragrant. Be careful not to burn them. Transfer toasted chiles to a bowl, cover with hot water or some of the reserved chicken broth, and let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until soft.

instructions.title: Roast Aromatics and Toast Spices
instructions.text: While chiles rehydrate, spread tomatoes, tomatillos, onion, and garlic on a baking sheet. Roast under a broiler or in a hot skillet until softened and lightly charred. In a separate dry skillet, toast almonds, pepitas, and sesame seeds until fragrant. Separately, toast the cinnamon stick, cloves, peppercorns, cumin seeds, and oregano until aromatic.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Base
instructions.text: Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving the soaking liquid. In a blender, combine the softened chiles, roasted tomatoes, tomatillos, onion, garlic, toasted almonds, pepitas, sesame seeds, toasted spices, and Mexican chocolate. Add 2 cups of the reserved chicken broth. Blend until very smooth, adding more broth or chile soaking liquid if needed to achieve a thick, pourable consistency.

instructions.title: Simmer the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: Heat lard or vegetable oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour the blended mole mixture into the hot oil. It will splatter. Bring to a simmer, then reduce heat to low and cook, stirring frequently, for at least 20-25 minutes. The sauce will thicken and darken, and the flavors will deepen. Season with salt and sugar to taste.

instructions.title: Combine Chicken and Serve
instructions.text: Add the cooked chicken pieces to the simmering mole sauce. Gently stir to coat the chicken completely. Continue to simmer for another 5-10 minutes to allow the flavors to meld and the chicken to heat through. Serve hot, garnished with extra sesame seeds if desired, alongside rice and warm tortillas.

tips: For a truly authentic mole, take your time to properly toast each ingredient individually. This develops deep, complex flavors that are essential to a good mole.
If you cant find all three types of dried chiles, you can substitute with more ancho or pasilla chiles. For a spicier mole, add a dried arbol chile or two to the blend.
Mole sauce freezes beautifully. Make a larger batch and freeze portions for quick meals later. It can be stored in an airtight container for up to 3 months.
article:
The rich, complex flavors of Mexican cuisine have captivated palates worldwide, and at the heart of its culinary marvels lies the enigmatic mole. Among its many iterations, Pollo en Mole, or Chicken Mole, stands out as a true masterpiece – a dish that embodies tradition, depth, and an unparalleled taste experience. Forget what you think you know about sauces; mole is a universe unto itself, a symphony of chiles, spices, nuts, and even chocolate, all harmoniously blended to create something truly magical.

If you've ever been intimidated by the thought of making mole from scratch, you're not alone. Its legendary complexity often deters home cooks. However, with the right guidance, creating an authentic and delicious Chicken Mole Recipe is not only achievable but incredibly rewarding. This article will demystify the process, guiding you through each step to bring this iconic Mexican chicken dish to your own kitchen. Prepare to embark on a culinary journey that promises to delight your senses and impress your guests with a truly unforgettable Chicken Mole Recipe.

## What is Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole)?

At its core, "mole" (pronounced MOH-lay) is a generic term for a variety of sauces in Mexican cuisine, derived from the Nahuatl word "mōlli," meaning "sauce." However, when most people refer to mole, they're often thinking of the rich, dark, and incredibly complex sauces that typically feature a blend of several dried chiles, spices, nuts, seeds, and often a touch of chocolate. Pollo en Mole, therefore, simply means chicken prepared in this exquisite sauce.

The history of mole is as rich and layered as its flavor, with origins stretching back to pre-Hispanic times. While there are countless regional variations – Mole Poblano, Mole Negro, Mole Amarillo, Mole Verde, to name a few – they all share the common thread of being labor-intensive, slow-cooked sauces designed to achieve an extraordinary depth of flavor. The most famous, Mole Poblano, is often associated with the state of Puebla and is characterized by its dark color and inclusion of chocolate.

What makes Pollo en Mole so special is the incredible balance it strikes between sweet, savory, spicy, and earthy notes. It's a testament to the art of balancing ingredients, where no single flavor dominates, but rather contributes to a harmonious whole. The chicken, usually simmered until fork-tender within the sauce, absorbs these magnificent flavors, transforming a simple protein into an elevated dining experience. Far from being just a meal, a good Chicken Mole Recipe is a celebration of Mexican culinary heritage.

## Ingredients You Need for Chicken Mole

Crafting an authentic Chicken Mole Recipe from scratch requires a diverse array of ingredients, each playing a crucial role in building its intricate flavor profile. Don't be overwhelmed by the list; many are pantry staples, and the specialized items are readily available in most Latin American markets or online.

For the Chicken:
*   **1 whole chicken (about 3-4 lbs)**, cut into 8-10 pieces, or 6-8 bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs and drumsticks.
*   **1 onion**, quartered.
*   **2-3 cloves garlic**, smashed.
*   **Salt** to taste.
*   **Water or chicken broth**.

For the Mole Sauce Base:
*   **Dried Chiles:**
    *   4-5 large **Ancho chiles** (for fruity, mild heat and dark color)
    *   2-3 large **Mulato chiles** (for smoky, earthy notes and dark color)
    *   2-3 large **Pasilla chiles** (for mild heat, fruity, and slightly licorice notes)
*   **Aromatics:**
    *   1 medium **white onion**, roughly chopped.
    *   4-5 cloves **garlic**, peeled.
*   **Spices:**
    *   1 teaspoon **cumin seeds**.
    *   1/2 teaspoon **coriander seeds**.
    *   1/4 teaspoon **whole black peppercorns**.
    *   1/4 teaspoon **cloves**.
    *   1 small stick (1-2 inches) **Mexican cinnamon** or canela.
    *   1-2 **star anise** (optional, for a subtle licorice note).
*   **Nuts and Seeds:**
    *   1/4 cup **blanched almonds**.
    *   1/4 cup **peanuts**.
    *   2 tablespoons **sesame seeds** (plus extra for garnish).
    *   2 tablespoons **pumpkin seeds (pepitas)**.
*   **Thickeners/Flavor Enhancers:**
    *   2 medium **Roma tomatoes**, roughly chopped.
    *   1/2 plantain (or 1 slice of bread/tortilla), fried until golden (for body and sweetness).
*   **Sweetener/Depth:**
    *   1 tablet (3 oz) **Mexican chocolate** (like Ibarra or Abuelita), broken into pieces. This is crucial for the authentic Chicken Mole Recipe.
    *   1-2 tablespoons **piloncillo** (Mexican brown sugar cone) or dark brown sugar, to taste.
*   **Fat:**
    *   2-3 tablespoons **lard or vegetable oil**.
*   **Liquid:**
    *   4-6 cups **chicken broth** (reserved from cooking chicken, or store-bought).
*   **Salt** to taste.

## Step-by-Step Instructions for Pollo en Mole

Making this Chicken Mole Recipe is a labor of love, but each step contributes to the magnificent end result.

1.  **Prepare the Chicken:** Place chicken pieces, quartered onion, smashed garlic, and a generous pinch of salt in a large pot. Cover with water or chicken broth. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat, cover, and simmer for 30-40 minutes, or until chicken is tender and cooked through. Remove chicken, strain and reserve the cooking broth. You'll need about 4-6 cups for the mole.
2.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Carefully remove stems and seeds from the dried chiles (Ancho, Mulato, Pasilla). Heat a dry comal or skillet over medium heat. Toast the chiles lightly for 30 seconds to 1 minute per side until fragrant but not burnt (they should be pliable, not crispy). Place toasted chiles in a bowl, cover with hot water or reserved chicken broth, and let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until softened.
3.  **Toast Nuts, Seeds & Spices:** In the same dry skillet, toast the almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds separately until fragrant and lightly browned. Be careful not to burn them. Remove and set aside. Then, lightly toast the cumin seeds, coriander seeds, peppercorns, cloves, cinnamon stick, and star anise (if using) for 30 seconds to 1 minute until fragrant.
4.  **Sauté Aromatics & Thickeners:** In a skillet with 1-2 tablespoons of lard or oil, sauté the chopped white onion and garlic until softened and translucent, about 5-7 minutes. Add the chopped Roma tomatoes and cook for another 5 minutes until softened. If using, fry the plantain or bread/tortilla slice until golden.
5.  **Blend the Mole Paste:** Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving some of the soaking liquid. In a powerful blender, combine the rehydrated chiles, sautéed onion and garlic, toasted spices, toasted nuts and seeds, tomatoes, fried plantain/bread, and about 1 cup of the reserved chicken broth. Blend until very smooth. This might take a few minutes, scraping down the sides as needed. Add more broth gradually if necessary to achieve a thick, smooth paste. For an exceptionally silky sauce, pass the blended paste through a fine-mesh sieve.
6.  **Simmer the Mole Sauce:** In a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven, heat the remaining 1-2 tablespoons of lard or oil over medium heat. Carefully pour in the blended mole paste. Be prepared for it to sputter. Stir constantly for 10-15 minutes, allowing the sauce to "fry" and deepen in color and flavor. This step is crucial for developing the mole's richness.
7.  **Add Chocolate and Broth:** Gradually stir in the Mexican chocolate and piloncillo (or brown sugar) until melted and fully incorporated. Begin adding the reserved chicken broth, 1 cup at a time, stirring constantly until the sauce reaches your desired consistency – it should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon, but still pourable. Bring to a gentle simmer, reduce heat to low, cover, and cook for at least 30-60 minutes, stirring occasionally, to allow the flavors to meld and deepen.
8.  **Combine Chicken and Sauce:** Add the cooked chicken pieces to the simmering mole sauce. Stir gently to coat the chicken thoroughly. Continue to simmer for another 15-20 minutes, allowing the chicken to absorb the rich flavors of the mole. Taste and adjust seasoning with salt and additional piloncillo/sugar if needed.

## Pro Tips for the Best Chicken Mole Sauce

Achieving an outstanding Chicken Mole Recipe goes beyond just following the steps; these pro tips will elevate your dish from good to unforgettable.

*   **Toasting is Non-Negotiable:** Every dry ingredient – chiles, nuts, seeds, and spices – must be toasted. This process awakens their essential oils and deepens their flavors. However, toast gently; burnt ingredients will impart a bitter taste that can ruin your mole. Work in batches if necessary.
*   **Invest in a Good Blender:** A powerful blender is your best friend for a truly smooth mole. If your blender isn't top-tier, don't hesitate to strain the blended sauce through a fine-mesh sieve after blending. This extra step ensures a luxurious, velvety texture.
*   **Patience is a Virtue:** Mole is not a fast food. The long simmering times, both for the initial sauce base and when the chicken is added, are essential for the flavors to meld and deepen. Rushing this process will result in a less complex and satisfying sauce. A good Chicken Mole Recipe rewards patience.
*   **Balance Your Flavors:** Taste, taste, taste! Mole is all about balance. After the initial simmer, taste your sauce. Does it need more salt? A touch more sweetness (from piloncillo or brown sugar) to round out the chile's bitterness? A splash of extra broth for consistency? Adjust as needed.
*   **Don't Fear the Chocolate:** Mexican chocolate in mole isn't for sweetness (though it does add a subtle hint). Its primary role is to add depth, richness, and a beautiful dark sheen, balancing the spice and earthiness of the chiles. Don't skip it!
*   **Make Ahead for Better Flavor:** Like many complex stews and sauces, mole often tastes even better the next day. The flavors continue to meld and deepen overnight. Consider making the sauce a day in advance and gently reheating it with the chicken on serving day.
*   **Right Consistency:** The ideal mole sauce should be thick enough to cling to the chicken and coat the back of a spoon but still flow. If it's too thick, add more chicken broth. If too thin, simmer uncovered for a bit longer.

## Serving Suggestions for Pollo en Mole

Once your magnificent Chicken Mole Recipe is complete, presenting it beautifully is the final touch. This rich and hearty dish pairs wonderfully with simple accompaniments that allow its complex flavors to shine.

*   **Warm Tortillas:** The quintessential pairing. Serve with a stack of freshly warmed corn tortillas (or flour tortillas, if preferred). They are perfect for scooping up every last drop of the luscious sauce.
*   **Mexican Rice:** A side of fluffy, vibrant red Mexican rice (arroz rojo) is a traditional and delicious accompaniment, providing a mild counterpoint to the mole's intensity.
*   **Refried Beans:** Creamy refried beans (frijoles refritos) offer another comforting and classic side that complements the mole beautifully.
*   **Simple Salad:** A light, refreshing green salad with a simple vinaigrette can cut through the richness of the mole, offering a pleasant contrast.
*   **Garnishes:** A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds (reserving some from the sauce ingredients), fresh cilantro, or a crumble of queso fresco adds texture, freshness, and visual appeal.
*   **Beverages:** Pair your Pollo en Mole with an agua fresca (such as jamaica or horchata), a crisp Mexican lager, or a reposado tequila for a complete culinary experience.

## Variations and Substitutions for Your Mole

While the classic Chicken Mole Recipe is a marvel, the beauty of mole lies in its adaptability. Once you've mastered the basic recipe, feel free to experiment with variations and substitutions to suit your taste or available ingredients.

*   **Chile Variations:** The trio of Ancho, Mulato, and Pasilla is classic, but you can experiment with others. Guajillo chiles add a fruity, tangy note, while a small amount of Chile de Arbol can significantly increase the heat level if you like a spicier mole.
*   **Nut and Seed Alternatives:** While almonds, peanuts, sesame, and pumpkin seeds are traditional, you can substitute or add other nuts like pecans, walnuts, or even hazelnuts for different flavor nuances.
*   **Chocolate Choices:** If Mexican chocolate is unavailable, a high-quality unsweetened or dark chocolate (70% cocoa or higher) can be used. Just be sure to adjust the piloncillo or brown sugar to achieve the desired balance, as Mexican chocolate often contains sugar and cinnamon.
*   **Protein Swaps:** While Pollo en Mole is the star, the sauce is incredibly versatile. It's excellent with turkey (Pavo en Mole), pork shoulder, or even beef short ribs. For a vegetarian option, try it with roasted vegetables, plantains, or firm tofu/tempeh.
*   **Sweetener Adjustments:** Piloncillo is traditional, but brown sugar works perfectly well. You can also add a touch of raisins or prunes to the blend for a natural sweetness and added depth.
*   **Shortcuts (with a caveat):** For those truly short on time, pre-made mole paste is available in jars or blocks. While convenient, these often lack the fresh, nuanced flavors of a homemade mole. If you use one, you'll still need to sauté it in oil and thin it with broth, and you might want to "doctor" it with some extra spices or chocolate. However, we highly encourage you to try making this Chicken Mole Recipe from scratch at least once!

Embracing the process of making Pollo en Mole from scratch is a culinary adventure that connects you to generations of Mexican tradition. It's a dish that speaks of patience, passion, and the incredible art of flavor layering. Don't let its reputation intimidate you; instead, let this guide empower you to create a truly authentic and delicious Chicken Mole Recipe in your own kitchen. The effort is well worth it, promising a deeply satisfying meal that will transport your taste buds straight to Mexico. So gather your ingredients, clear your schedule, and prepare to experience the magic of homemade Pollo en Mole.
